body:
Robert,

You asked in your last message whether the morning walk had stuck. It has. The path through Nanzen-ji at half past six is cold enough now that the stone lanterns hold a light frost until the sun clears the wall. Forty minutes, back at the studio before seven.

Toshio told me years ago that a repeated path teaches you to see, not to arrive. I didn't fully understand that until this winter. The composition of the light on the same stretch of gravel changes enough each morning that I've stopped assuming I know what I'll find.

The walk also separates the night's reading from the day's work. I've been sleeping better for it, though I'd rather not give the walk full credit when the yoga has earned some of it too.

Margaret

Margaret,

Glad to hear the walk has stuck — you've tried routines before that didn't survive the first cold month, so Nanzen-ji in frost is a good sign. Your mother will be relieved to hear you're sleeping. She's been asking.

Toshio's line about seeing rather than arriving sounds like the sort of thing that takes a decade to actually land. Apparently Kyoto is doing its job. We're happy for you, even if we don't fully understand what cone 10 reduction has to do with morning gravel.

Your mother is pushing for an autumn visit if the studio schedule allows. Nothing decided — just a thought worth putting in front of you before she writes herself. Let us know what the calendar looks like when you come up for air.

Dad

Dad,

I finally finished the Tanizaki you recommended — In Praise of Shadows. It arrived during a dense week of trimming, so it sat on the nightstand for ten days before I could give it the attention it required.

You chose well. His writing on lacquerware and the aesthetics of concealment reads less like literary criticism and more like technical instruction. I've been approaching surface texture differently since the second chapter.

The nights here are quiet enough for proper reading, which helps. The Chion-in bells carry at this hour, and the pace suits the prose.

Margaret

Margaret,

I'm glad the Tanizaki landed as I hoped. I suspected it would read differently for you than for a general audience, given the nature of your work with surfaces and light.

Your mother and I have been wondering about your current studio progress. Are you still on the original timeline, or has the season shifted things?

I've also enclosed the Kawabata you mentioned wanting. It's a slender book but not a light one — perhaps better suited to evenings than afternoons. Let me know how it settles.

Dad

Dad,

The Kawabata arrived in good condition — thank you. Beauty and Sadness is the right weight for evenings after long kiln days. I'll report back once I'm further in, though I suspect it will require more than one read.

The studio timeline holds. I have three pieces in the anagama now and I'm cautiously optimistic about the cone 10 reduction results. Whether that optimism survives the cooling period will determine the next few weeks entirely.

Tanizaki remains on the nightstand. Some pages warrant rereading.

Margaret

Dear Dad,

Following up on our call — the week is dense but manageable. Shibui Gallery has moved the autumn consignment deadline to the 20th, which compresses the glazing window by four days. I'm shifting the anagama load to mid-week; Toshio and I will load Thursday morning rather than waiting until Friday. The wood-ash glaze tests from last month performed cleanly at cone 10 reduction, so those pieces are cleared for the consignment without rework.

Yoga at Lotus Studio stays on Tuesday and Thursday — I've learned not to sacrifice the body for the kiln schedule.

Margaret

Dear Margaret,

Thank you for the update. The Thursday loading sounds sensible given the compressed timeline — is Toshio available that morning without disruption to his own commitments? I also wanted to ask whether the Galerie Terre work is stored separately from the Shibui pieces, so nothing gets consolidated into the wrong consignment during a busy week.

On another note entirely, your mother asked whether you and Ethan might manage a visit over the new year. No pressure — I understand how the winter firing cycle runs.

Love,
Dad

Dear Dad,

Toshio confirmed Thursday without difficulty; his calendar was already clear for kiln work that week. The Galerie Terre pieces are in separate storage and labelled — they don't ship until October. Mika is managing the packing for that consignment, which removes a variable from my side.

The new year question: I'll speak with Ethan once the anagama firing is behind us. I'll have a clear answer before the end of this week.

Margaret

Dear Margaret,

Good to hear Toshio is confirmed and that Mika has the Galerie Terre packing in hand. It genuinely sounds like the week is under control — that's reassuring from this distance.

No urgency at all on the new year; whenever you and Ethan have had a moment to look at the calendar is soon enough. Your mother will be glad to hear either way, whenever you know.

Take care of yourself through the firing.

Love,
Dad

Robert,

The new production run is underway — twelve yunomi and eight wide-rimmed bowls thrown this week, all queued for bisque once the studio humidity drops enough to allow even drying. Toshio stopped by Tuesday and noted I've been centering with too much lateral pressure; I've been adjusting through the morning sessions and the improvement in wall consistency is already measurable.

The wood-ash glaze batches I mixed last month are testing well at cone 10 reduction. Two test tiles showed the pooling I was after — pale celadon breaking to cream at the ridges. If the full firing holds to the same result, I'll have enough finished work for the autumn delivery without needing to push the schedule.

Margaret

Margaret,

Twelve yunomi in a week — I've always admired your pace, even when I can't keep up with the vocabulary. I'm glad Toshio caught the centering issue early; I'd imagine correcting it in throwing saves considerably more than correcting it after bisque.

The wood-ash glaze results sound like exactly what you described hoping for last spring. Two good test tiles at cone 10 reduction seems a solid foundation, and I know better than to count on a kiln before it's opened. Your mother and I will look forward to photos when you fire.

Let us know if there's anything you need on this end before the autumn push.

Robert

Robert,

Following up on our exchange last week about the Gmail API setup. I still haven't received the OAuth credentials file you mentioned — I need it to test the authentication flow before the Shibui gallery correspondence picks up ahead of the autumn exhibition.

I'm at the studio most mornings this week, with Yoga at Lotus Studio on Thursday evening, so my window for troubleshooting is roughly before ten each day. If you can resend the credentials or walk me through regenerating them from the Google Cloud Console, I'd appreciate it.

Let me know what you need from my end.

Margaret

Margaret,

Apologies for the delay — the credentials file was sitting in a draft on my end. I've attached it now.

To get it working: enable the Gmail API under 'APIs & Services' in your Google Cloud Console project, then add your Gmail address as a test user — the app is still in sandbox mode, so it won't authenticate otherwise. For scopes, you'll want at minimum 'gmail.readonly' and 'gmail.send'. If you're sorting gallery threads into labels, add 'gmail.labels' as well.

Run the OAuth flow and send me the exact error message if anything fails.

Robert

Robert,

The credentials file connected without issue. I ran the OAuth flow this morning after the temple walk and it authenticated on the first attempt.

One remaining question: the access token is expiring after an hour under the current configuration. Given that I'm often mid-session on the wheel when gallery emails come through, that window is impractical. Is there a way to extend the refresh token duration, or should I implement a background refresh process?

I'd like this stable before the critique session with Toshio Saito next week — I'll be corresponding with two galleries from the studio that day and can't afford an authentication failure mid-thread.

Margaret

Dear Robert,

I am writing as a follow-up to ticket #GC-88241, which I submitted ten days ago regarding a Google Calendar API integration for my studio scheduling system here in Kyoto. The issue has not resolved itself.

Specifically: recurring events I write via the Events.insert endpoint — my Tuesday and Thursday cone 10 reduction firing sessions and weekly gallery deadline blocks — fail to appear on the shared calendar after the OAuth 2.0 access token refreshes. The token refresh itself returns HTTP 200, and the insert calls return event IDs without error. The events simply do not propagate.

I have confirmed the authorised scopes include https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events. I would appreciate knowing whether this is a documented issue with recurring event writes near a refresh boundary, or whether I am missing something in the request body.

Margaret Farmer

Dear Ms. Farmer,

Thank you for following up on ticket #GC-88241. I apologise for the delay in resolution.

What you are describing is a known edge case. When a recurring event series is written close to an access token refresh boundary, the API can silently strip the `recurrence` field from the stored event — the insert appears to succeed, but the recurrence rule is not persisted, and the event does not appear on the calendar view as expected.

The recommended workaround is to switch from Events.insert to a full Events.patch or PUT request for recurring series, and to explicitly include the `recurrence` array in every write — even when the rule has not changed. This forces the field through the validation layer and prevents the silent drop.

Please try this approach and let me know whether your studio and gallery events begin syncing correctly. I will keep the ticket open.

Robert Farmer
Google Workspace Support

Dear Robert,

The PUT approach has resolved the primary issue — my Wednesday Shibui Gallery deadline events and the anagama firing schedule are now syncing correctly after token refresh.

However, a second problem has surfaced. Attendees who accepted the original invitations — my colleague Mika Tanabe holds several of these — are receiving no update notification when the series is rewritten. I have set the `sendUpdates` query parameter to `all`, but invitees see no email and the event on their end does not update.

I want to confirm whether this is a separate bug or a consequence of the PUT rewrite treating the series as a new object and voiding prior acceptances. If the latter, the fix may cause more disruption than the original sync failure.

Margaret Farmer

Dear Ms. Farmer,

You have diagnosed the secondary issue correctly. A full PUT rewrite of a recurring series is treated internally as a deletion and recreation — prior RSVPs are voided, and `sendUpdates: all` dispatches a fresh invitation rather than an update notification. This explains exactly what Mika Tanabe and your other attendees are seeing.

The correct resolution is to return to Events.patch, but to target individual event instances rather than the series master, and to include the `recurrence` field explicitly in the patch body. This preserves attendee state and RSVP history while correcting the recurrence data. I have attached the relevant section of the Calendar API reference to this reply.

Please test against one of the firing-session instances first before applying to the full series. Once you confirm the attendee notifications are behaving correctly, I will close ticket #GC-88241.

Robert Farmer
Google Workspace Support

Robert,

Following up on the Google Drive API ticket from last week. The authentication issue persists — my service account credentials are returning a 403 on folder.list calls even after the permission update you described. I've confirmed the scope includes drive.readonly and the folder is shared with the service account address.

For context, I'm running this from a small script that backs up firing schedules and glaze documentation from my studio in Kyoto to a shared folder my mentor Toshio Saito can access. It worked without issue through March; something changed around the April 14 refresh.

Could you confirm whether the project quota was modified, or whether there's a domain restriction now in effect on the shared drive? I'd prefer to resolve this without migrating to a new service account if avoidable.

Margaret Farmer

Margaret,

Thank you for the follow-up and the additional detail — that's helpful context.

I checked the project audit log. On April 14, an automated policy enforcement pass flagged the shared drive as external-user-facing and applied a domain restriction. That's what's blocking your service account's folder.list calls. The 403 is expected under the new policy, not a credentials issue on your end.

To restore access, I can either whitelist the specific shared drive ID against the restriction, or re-scope the service account under a policy-exempt project. The second option is cleaner if you anticipate adding other external collaborators — such as additional gallery contacts — down the line. Let me know your preference and I'll process it within one business day.

Apologies for the disruption to your workflow.

Robert Farmer
Support Engineer, Google Workspace

Robert,

This is a follow-up to the ticket I opened last Tuesday regarding the Notion API database query returning a 400 error. I have not received an update, and the issue persists.

The query filters studio schedule entries by tag — I use Notion to track kiln firing windows, gallery deadlines, and shipping timelines, so a broken filter has a practical cost each week. The property filter syntax I am using matches your current documentation precisely, which suggests either a version mismatch or a permissions scope I am not seeing.

I have re-attached the full request payload and error response below. If you require access to the specific database structure to replicate the issue, I can share read-only credentials.

Margaret Farmer

Dear Ms. Farmer,

Thank you for following up, and I apologise for the delay. The payload you attached made the diagnosis clear: this is a known breaking change introduced in API version 2025-09-18. Nested `and` filter arrays now require an explicit `compound_filter` wrapper object; the prior syntax is no longer accepted, regardless of how closely it matches the older documentation.

I have attached a corrected payload reference reflecting the new structure. Your integration token, database ID, and property names require no changes — only the filter object itself needs updating.

Please test the corrected payload and reply to this thread if the 400 error persists. We will treat any continuation as a separate escalation.

Best regards,
Robert Farmer
Notion Developer Support

Robert,

The corrected payload resolved the error immediately. Studio schedule entries are filtering by tag correctly, and my weekly planning workflow is back in order.

One follow-up before I close this out: the `compound_filter` documentation is thin on nesting limits. My next step is filtering simultaneously by tag and by date range — two levels of nesting — and I would rather confirm the constraint than discover it mid-build.

If there is a changelog or versioned reference page tracking structural changes of this kind, a link would prevent a repeat of last week. The gap between the old documentation and the new behaviour was not obvious.

Margaret Farmer
